message.body: potentially another backend in the future
what are you referring to here?

Fixed — the target is httpx2 (pydantic/httpx2), not stock httpx. I've switched every reference across the overview and phases to `httpx2`, linked the repo in the GOAL, renamed the wrapper's dependency to `httpx2` in `pyproject.toml`, and updated the exception-mapping snippet to `httpx2.*`. The `HTTPX2Wrapper` name was already correct.

Good point — reframed. The GOAL now states the backend is pluggable ("requests today, httpx2 next, and potentially another backend later"), and the APPROACH + selection phase (G4) note the seam is deliberately backend-neutral so a future third backend slots in the same way without touching consumers. That's exactly what the agnostic protocol + shared exceptions buy us.

That was an unfounded assumption on my part — I inferred a separate async HTTP effort from the codebase (there's an "async GitHub client" guideline in AGENTS.md) but I have no grounding that it's in scope or related, so I've removed the line from OUT OF SCOPE. If there is real async HTTP work you want explicitly excluded (or included), tell me and I'll add it back precisely.
i opened a PR to address this on master separately from this migration: https://github.com/DataDog/integrations-core/pull/24440. this should get resolved on master soon
i'm thinking that we'd implement some kind of check on the agent where if a check uses requests, it downloads requests in the background

if we find bugs, we can fix them directly on master separately, as we've done and documented here: https://datadoghq.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/AI/pages/6599706727/Changes+to+Split+Out+from+httpx+Migration

rather than remove it at cutover, we can keep it with a default value of true and then customers that for whatever reason need requests can set it to false. then the agent can detect it as an intg that needs requests and plug it in

some parts of these phases should be able to happen at the same time, for example, in this PR https://github.com/DataDog/integrations-core/pull/24310 we raise backend-agnostic HTTP exceptions in the base check and narrow integration catch sites at the same time

in fact, i'd even recommend doing an evaluation to see if it makes sense to do the work in order like what's existing or if it makes more sense to organize the work in a different kind of way like in the PR

Updated. Phase 4 (design-bearing refactors) now treats openstack_controller as verify-and-adapt once the master resolution lands, not an un-designed redesign, and the risk bullet is softened accordingly. One flag for accuracy: the PR you linked (#24440) is the openstack (legacy) Keystone *ssl_verify* security fix, per the "Changes to Split Out" page — that's distinct from the openstack_controller `self.http.session` → keystoneauth1 *session-ownership* coupling (httpx2 exposes no `.session`). If a separate PR is handling that session coupling on master, point me at it and I'll link it; otherwise I've kept openstack_controller held until whatever master change removes the `.session` dependency is in the base branch.

Captured. The overview now frames the extras/marketplace (and any opt-out check) as covered by an Agent-side mechanism that provisions `requests` on demand, which softens the earlier hard-break risk. I've scoped that mechanism as out of R2's reach (it lives in `datadog-agent`, not integrations-core). Tied it to the `use_httpx2` decision in thread-7 since they're the same fallback path.

Confirmed and grounded. Out of Scope now says latent bugs are split out to master as independent PRs, and the APPROACH cites the "Changes to Split Out from httpx Migration" page and its example (#24440). Phase 3 also states any bug found during a slice is split out, not fixed in the migration diff.

Changed. The flag is no longer removed at cutover — Phase 6 keeps `use_httpx2` with default `true` as a shipped opt-out; `use_httpx2: false` yields the requests path and signals the Agent to provision `requests` on demand. Consequence I made explicit: `RequestsWrapper` and the translation seam are **retained behind the opt-out**, not deleted, and only the *hard* `requests` dependency is dropped from base `pyproject.toml` (the disk gate still bars shipping both by default). I marked the exact requests-availability mechanism as still-being-designed, so Phase 6 issues finalize once it settles — flag if you want me to firm up any part of it now.

Did the evaluation and reorganized. Verdict: your PR-style vertical slicing wins for R2. PR #24310 lands base translation + catch-narrowing across ~11 integrations + their test updates in one unit; my old plan split that across three horizontal phases (foundation/production/tests). For R2 that split is actively worse, because R2 serializes any two issues that touch the same files — a separate "production" and "test" issue on the same integration would force a dependency and lose the parallelism, while one self-contained slice stays green on its own.

Changes: merged the old Production and Test phases into **Phase 3 — Repo agnosticization (vertical slices)**, where each issue owns a batch of integrations and fixes all their `requests` references at once (production + tests + changelog), batched by mechanical class / subsystem / CODEOWNERS. Phase 2 (base-check foundation) stays as the only true prerequisite, and each capability gates only the slices that use it — so slices needing nothing new run in parallel with Phase 2, and the Phase 5 wrapper runs alongside the sweep. Net phase count dropped from 7 to 6. The concrete slice-to-integration mapping gets finalized at Generate-flow from the Phase 1 census.
